Detonation spray coating (DSC) deposition creates dense microstructures with excellent inter-splat bonding, enabling rapid formation of protective thin NiO layers with NiCr₂O₄ spinels; this dense structure limits salt penetration (22 ± 4 μm corrosion depth) and enhances microhardness (421 ± 54 HV0.2) by preventing corrosive species ingress. Conversely, atmospheric plasma spray (APS) deposition yields porous coatings with inter-splat boundaries, acting as fast diffusion paths for corrosive species, leading to mixed NiO/Co₃O₄ oxides with NiCr₂O₄ spinels and deeper corrosion (86 ± 11 μm). For bond coats, HVAF spraying followed by 1100°C heat treatment forms a β-NiAl + γ-NiCoCr two-phase microstructure; the β-phase promotes α-Al₂O₃ formation, but interfacial voids at the oxide/bond coat interface reduce adhesion, causing oxide scale spallation above 1050°C. Sulfur segregation at the thermally grown oxide (TGO)/metal interface, exacerbated by coarse γ/β phase structures with inhomogeneous Y/Hf distribution, further reduces interfacial toughness and drives spallation. Fine columnar Al₂O₃ grains (1.1 ± 0.2 μm) increase oxygen diffusion, raising oxidation rates (2.6 × 10⁻² μm²/h), while interface rumpling from β→γ phase transformation creates tensile stress concentrations, promoting severe spallation (>50%). Processing methods (SPS, arc-melting, quenching) directly control phase distribution and oxide scale continuity, which govern oxidation resistance at 1150°C through diffusion barriers and stress management.

The oxidation resistance of **Ni39.836Cr17.9262Co17.9262Al23.9016Hf0.07Y0.34** is **preserved at 1000°C** due to:  
1. **Slower oxidation kinetics** (\( K_p \) reduction).
